exemplar.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained focus on the prescribed title and clearly defines key terms (model, wrong, useful).
Provides concrete and relevant examples from two areas of knowledge (natural sciences and mathematics).
Demonstrates an emergent awareness of alternative viewpoints by acknowledging counterclaims.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive with limited depth in explaining how and why examples illuminate the knowledge question.
Minimal critical evaluation of implications and knowledge frameworks; lacks sustained comparison of contrasting perspectives.
Essayâs reasoning sometimes relies on assertion without robust evidential support and misses opportunities to explore the limits and refinement of models.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official total of 3/10 reflects very limited analytical depth despite clear structure and relevant examples. While the essay sustains focus on the title and defines terms accurately, its largely descriptive treatment and lack of sustained critical evaluation constrain its performance to the lower end of the mark range. Distributing all 3 marks to the single gradable criterion acknowledges the basic strengths in structure and relevance, while the pervasive weaknesses in depth and evaluation justify the low overall score.

annotations.comment: The assertion that matter âmay be composed of something entirely differentâ introduces speculation without evidence or linkage to real-world examples, weakening the argumentâs reliability.
Discussion of empirical scope is insightful but general; the student should provide specific experimental data or real-world deviations to illustrate where the model fails.
The link between empirical knowledge and model correctness needs more depth; consider discussing how repeated experiments refine models and the limits of induction.
This summary repeats earlier points without new insight; the student should integrate perspectives and evaluate the significance of Boxâs claim for knowledge advancement.
While the student defines âmodel,â âwrong,â and âuseful,â these definitions remain descriptive. They should link definitions explicitly to knowledge frameworks to deepen analysis.
This opening definition of natural science models is clear but descriptive; the student should connect explicitly to TOK concepts (e.g., ways of knowing) to deepen insight.
The atomic scale is quantified but the notation â<math>1 \times 10^{-10}</math>â is not formatted in LaTeX. It should appear as $$1\times10^{-10}\,\text{m}$$ for clarity.
This paragraph would benefit from comparing alternative viewpoints, such as instances where models misled research, to demonstrate a balanced evaluation of usefulness.
The student should explicitly relate this example back to the prescribed title by analysing the implications of wrong yet useful models on knowledge production in the natural sciences.
The shift to the mathematics section is abrupt; the student should introduce how mathematical modelling differs epistemically from scientific modelling, clarifying distinctions between these AoKs.
The prescribed title is clearly restated but the student could frame it more explicitly as a knowledge question to guide the essayâs inquiry, improving coherence and focus.
The introduction establishes context but does not explicitly formulate a knowledge question. Articulating a precise KQ would sharpen the essayâs analytical direction.
The engineering example is relevant, but the student could deepen evaluation by discussing specific consequences of modelling errors, such as safety margins or design failures.
Figure 2 illustrates a perfect cube but the student does not discuss how mathematical ideals influence real-world modelling and the tension between abstraction and application.
The counterclaim that some models can be âcorrectâ is introduced but lacks development. The student should contrast contexts to evaluate this view more critically.
Figure 1 is informative historically, but the student misses an analysis of how each successive atomic model improved or limited understanding, which would strengthen evaluation of usefulness.
The claim that science âprobably would not have progressedâ lacks specific evidence; the student should cite cases where atomic models directly enabled particular technological breakthroughs.
The concluding sentence generalises that 3D models are wrong without exploring degrees of error; the student should consider how tolerance levels affect model usefulness.
This discussion of 3D models is practical but remains descriptive; to strengthen the argument, the student should examine the underlying mathematical abstractions that lead to model inaccuracy.
This reflection lacks explicit linkage to the prescribed title; the student should reframe insights on certainty in knowledge directly in context of Boxâs claim.
The conclusion restates the thesis but offers limited synthesis; the student should draw out key implications for how knowledge is produced through models across AoKs.
The forward-looking comment on future 3D printing adds interest but lacks critical evaluation of current technological limitations, diluting analytical rigor.
The conclusion would benefit from evaluating the essayâs own limitations and proposing areas for further inquiry to demonstrate self-reflection and depth.
The exploration of the Ideal Gas Model is presented as a counterclaim, but the student should explicitly contrast it with earlier examples to evaluate broader implications.

The references include credible sources spanning both historical and contemporary perspectives. Incorporating more peer-reviewed literature would further strengthen the evidential support for key claims.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
